mn36:1.2: At one time the *Bhagavā* was dwelling at Vesālī, in the Great Wood, in the hall with the peaked roof.
mn36:10.1: “I am pleased in this way with Master Gotama.
mn36:10.2: For Master Gotama is one with developed body [*bhāvitakāyo*] and developed mind [*bhāvitacitto*].”
mn36:10.3: “Indeed, Aggivessana, that statement of yours has been spoken closely and directly, but I will answer you.
mn36:10.4: And I will explain it to you.
mn36:10.5: From the time, Aggivessana, when I shaved off hair and beard, put on the ochre robes, and went forth from the home life into homelessness, it is impossible that either an arisen pleasant *vedanā* should take possession of my mind [*citta*], or an arisen painful *vedanā* should take possession of my mind [*citta*].”
mn36:11.1: “Surely Master Gotama does not have such a pleasant *vedanā* arise that, when arisen, it could take possession of the mind [*citta*]; surely Master Gotama does not have such a painful *vedanā* arise that, when arisen, it could take possession of the mind [*citta*].”
mn36:12.1: “Why should that not be so, Aggivessana?
mn36:12.2: Here, Aggivessana, before my awakening, while I was still not fully awakened, being only the Bodhisatta, this thought occurred to me:
mn36:12.3: ‘The household life is cramped, a dusty path; going forth is the open air.
mn36:12.4: It is not easy, while living at home, to live the holy life utterly complete, utterly pure, polished like a shell.
mn36:12.5: What if I were to shave off hair and beard, put on the ochre robes, and go forth from the home life into homelessness?’
mn36:13.1: Then, Aggivessana, at a later time, while still young, a black-haired young man endowed with the blessing of youth, in the prime of life, though my mother and father wished otherwise, with tearful faces weeping, I shaved off hair and beard, put on the ochre robes, and went forth from the home life into homelessness.
mn36:13.10: ‘Āḷāra Kālāma does not declare, “I dwell having entered upon and realized this *dhamma* for myself by direct knowledge,” merely out of faith alone.
mn36:13.11: Surely Āḷāra Kālāma dwells knowing and seeing this *dhamma*.’
mn36:13.12: Then, Aggivessana, I approached Āḷāra Kālāma, and having approached him, I said this to him:
mn36:13.13: ‘Friend Kālāma, to what extent do you declare: “I dwell having entered upon and realized this *dhamma* for myself by direct knowledge”?’
mn36:13.14: When this was said, Aggivessana, Āḷāra Kālāma declared the dimension [*āyatana*] of nothingness [*ākiñcaññāyatanaṁ*].
mn36:13.15: Then this thought occurred to me, Aggivessana:
mn36:13.16: ‘It is not only Āḷāra Kālāma who has faith; I too have faith.
mn36:13.17: It is not only Āḷāra Kālāma who has energy; I too have energy.
mn36:13.18: It is not only Āḷāra Kālāma who has *sati*; I too have *sati*.
mn36:13.19: It is not only Āḷāra Kālāma who has *samādhi*; I too have *samādhi*.
mn36:13.2: Having thus gone forth, Aggivessana, seeking what is skillful, searching for the unsurpassed supreme state of peace, I approached Āḷāra Kālāma. Having approached him, I said this to Āḷāra Kālāma:
mn36:13.20: It is not only Āḷāra Kālāma who has discernment [*paññā*]; I too have discernment [*paññā*].
mn36:13.21: What if I were to strive for the realization of that *dhamma* which Āḷāra Kālāma declares that he dwells having realized for himself by direct knowledge?’
mn36:13.22: Then, Aggivessana, before long, very quickly, I dwelled having entered upon and realized that *dhamma* for myself by direct knowledge.
mn36:13.3: ‘Friend Kālāma, I wish to live the holy life in this *dhamma* and *vinaya* [*dhammavinaye*].’
mn36:13.4: When this was said, Aggivessana, Āḷāra Kālāma said this to me:
mn36:13.6: Such is this *dhamma* that a wise person may soon realize for himself, by direct knowledge, his teacher’s own doctrine and dwell having entered upon it.’
mn36:13.7: Then, Aggivessana, before long, very quickly, I learned that *dhamma*.
mn36:13.8: Then, Aggivessana, merely by lip-recitation and verbal repetition, I spoke a doctrine of knowledge and an elder’s doctrine, and I claimed, ‘I know, I see’—I, as well as others.
mn36:13.9: Then this thought occurred to me, Aggivessana:
mn36:14.1: Then, Aggivessana, I approached Āḷāra Kālāma, and having approached him, I said this to him:
mn36:14.10: As I am, so are you; as you are, so am I.
mn36:14.11: Come now, friend, let us both lead this group together.’
mn36:14.12: Thus, Aggivessana, though Āḷāra Kālāma was my teacher, he placed me, his own pupil, on the same level as himself, and honored me with the highest honor.
mn36:14.13: Then this thought occurred to me, Aggivessana:
mn36:14.14: ‘This *dhamma* does not lead to disenchantment, to dispassion, to cessation, to peace, to direct knowledge, to awakening, to *nibbāna*, but only to rebirth in the dimension [*āyatana*] of nothingness [*ākiñcaññāyatanūpapattiyā*].’
mn36:14.15: Then, Aggivessana, not delighting in that *dhamma*, becoming disenchanted with that *dhamma*, I departed.
mn36:14.2: ‘Friend Kālāma, is this the extent to which you declare: “I dwell having entered upon and realized this *dhamma* for myself by direct knowledge”?’
mn36:14.3: ‘Friend, this is the extent to which I declare: “I dwell having entered upon and realized this *dhamma* for myself by direct knowledge.”’
mn36:14.4: ‘Friend, I too dwell having entered upon and realized this *dhamma* for myself by direct knowledge, to this same extent.’
mn36:14.5: ‘It is a gain for us, friend, well gained for us, friend,
mn36:14.6: that we see such a venerable companion in the holy life.
mn36:14.7: So the *dhamma* that I declare after realizing it for myself by direct knowledge and entering upon it, that same *dhamma* you dwell in after realizing it for yourself by direct knowledge and entering upon it.
mn36:14.8: And the *dhamma* that you dwell in after realizing it for yourself by direct knowledge and entering upon it, that same *dhamma* I declare after realizing it for myself by direct knowledge and entering upon it.
mn36:14.9: So the *dhamma* that I know, that you know; and the *dhamma* that you know, that I know.
mn36:15.1: Then, Aggivessana, seeking what is skillful, searching for the unsurpassed supreme state of peace, I approached Udaka Rāmaputta. Having approached him, I said this to Udaka Rāmaputta:
mn36:15.10: Surely Rāma dwelt knowing and seeing this *dhamma*.’
mn36:15.11: Then, Aggivessana, I approached Udaka Rāmaputta, and having approached him, I said this to him:
mn36:15.12: ‘To what extent, friend, did Rāma declare: “I dwell having entered upon and realized this *dhamma* for myself by direct knowledge”?’
mn36:15.13: When this was said, Aggivessana, Udaka Rāmaputta declared the dimension [*āyatana*] of neither saññā nor non-saññā [*nevasaññānāsaññāyatanaṁ*].
mn36:15.14: Then this thought occurred to me, Aggivessana:
mn36:15.15: ‘It was not only Rāma who had faith; I too have faith.
mn36:15.16: It was not only Rāma who had energy; I too have energy.
mn36:15.17: It was not only Rāma who had *sati*; I too have *sati*.
mn36:15.18: It was not only Rāma who had *samādhi*; I too have *samādhi*.
mn36:15.19: It was not only Rāma who had discernment [*paññā*]; I too have discernment [*paññā*].
mn36:15.2: ‘Friend, I wish to live the holy life in this *dhamma* and *vinaya* [*dhammavinaye*].’
mn36:15.20: What if I were to strive for the realization of that *dhamma* which Rāma declared that he dwelt having realized for himself by direct knowledge?’
mn36:15.21: Then, Aggivessana, before long, very quickly, I dwelled having entered upon and realized that *dhamma* for myself by direct knowledge.
mn36:15.22: Then, Aggivessana, I approached Udaka Rāmaputta, and having approached him, I said this to him:
mn36:15.23: ‘Is this the extent, friend, to which Rāma declared: “I dwell having entered upon and realized this *dhamma* for myself by direct knowledge”?’
mn36:15.24: ‘Friend, this is the extent to which Rāma declared: “I dwell having entered upon and realized this *dhamma* for myself by direct knowledge.”’
mn36:15.25: ‘Friend, I too dwell having entered upon and realized this *dhamma* for myself by direct knowledge to this same extent.’
mn36:15.26: ‘It is a gain for us, friend, well gained for us, friend,
mn36:15.27: that we see such a venerable companion in the holy life.
mn36:15.28: So the *dhamma* that Rāma declared after realizing it for himself by direct knowledge and entering upon it, that same *dhamma* you dwell in after realizing it for yourself by direct knowledge and entering upon it; and the *dhamma* that you dwell in after realizing it for yourself by direct knowledge and entering upon it, that same *dhamma* Rāma declared after realizing it for himself by direct knowledge and entering upon it.
mn36:15.29: So the *dhamma* that Rāma directly knew, that *dhamma* you know; and the *dhamma* that you know, that *dhamma* Rāma directly knew.
mn36:15.3: When this was said, Aggivessana, Udaka Rāmaputta said this to me:
mn36:15.30: As Rāma was, so are you; as you are, so was Rāma.
mn36:15.31: Come now, friend, you lead this group.’
mn36:15.32: Thus, Aggivessana, Udaka Rāmaputta, though only my companion in the holy life, placed me in the teacher’s position and honored me with the highest honor.
mn36:15.33: Then this thought occurred to me, Aggivessana:
mn36:15.34: ‘This *dhamma* does not lead to disenchantment, to dispassion, to cessation, to peace, to direct knowledge, to awakening, to *nibbāna*, but only to rebirth in the dimension [*āyatana*] of neither saññā nor non-saññā [*nevasaññānāsaññāyatanūpapattiyā*].’
mn36:15.35: Then, Aggivessana, not delighting in that *dhamma*, becoming disenchanted with that *dhamma*, I departed.
mn36:15.5: Such is this *dhamma* that a wise person may soon realize for himself, by direct knowledge, his teacher’s own doctrine and dwell having entered upon it.’
mn36:15.6: Then, Aggivessana, before long, very quickly, I learned that *dhamma*.
mn36:15.7: Then, Aggivessana, merely by lip-recitation and verbal repetition, I spoke a doctrine of knowledge and an elder’s doctrine, and I claimed, ‘I know, I see’—I, as well as others.
mn36:15.8: Then this thought occurred to me, Aggivessana:
mn36:15.9: ‘Rāma does not declare, “I dwell having entered upon and realized this *dhamma* for myself by direct knowledge,” merely out of faith alone.
mn36:16.1: Then, Aggivessana, seeking what is skillful, searching for the unsurpassed supreme state of peace, wandering gradually on tour through the Magadhan country, I arrived at Uruvelā, at the village of Senānigama.
mn36:16.2: There I saw a delightful stretch of land, a lovely woodland grove, and a river flowing with clear water, with beautiful fordable banks, and nearby a village for alms-round.
mn36:16.3: Then this thought occurred to me, Aggivessana:
mn36:16.4: ‘How delightful indeed is this stretch of land, and lovely this woodland grove, and the river flows clear, with beautiful fordable banks, and nearby there is a village for alms-round.
mn36:16.5: This is enough for a clansman intent on striving.’
mn36:16.6: Then, Aggivessana, I sat down right there,
mn36:17.1: Then, Aggivessana, three similes occurred to me, never heard before, astonishing.
mn36:17.10: The man would only reap weariness and frustration.”
mn36:17.11: “In the same way, Aggivessana, whatever ascetics or brahmins dwell not withdrawn from sensual pleasures in body and mind, and whose sensual desire, sensual affection, sensual infatuation, sensual thirst, and sensual fever regarding sensual pleasures are not well abandoned and not well calmed internally—even if those good ascetics and brahmins feel painful, sharp, severe, bitter *vedanā* brought about by exertion, they are incapable of knowledge and vision and unsurpassed awakening.
mn36:17.12: And even if those good ascetics and brahmins do not feel painful, sharp, severe, bitter *vedanā* brought about by exertion, they are still incapable of knowledge and vision and unsurpassed awakening.
mn36:17.13: This was the first simile that occurred to me, Aggivessana, never heard before, astonishing.
mn36:17.2: Suppose, Aggivessana, there were wet, sappy wood lying in the water.
mn36:17.3: Then a man might come, taking an upper fire-stick:
mn36:17.4: ‘I will kindle fire, I will bring forth heat.’
mn36:17.6: Could that man, by rubbing that wet, sappy wood lying in the water with an upper fire-stick, kindle fire or bring forth heat?”
mn36:17.9: “Because, Master Gotama, that is wet, sappy wood, and it is lying in the water.
mn36:18.1: And then a second simile occurred to me, Aggivessana, never heard before, astonishing.
mn36:18.10: The man would only reap weariness and frustration.”
mn36:18.11: “In the same way, Aggivessana, whatever ascetics or brahmins dwell withdrawn from sensual pleasures in body and mind, but whose sensual desire, sensual affection, sensual infatuation, sensual thirst, and sensual fever regarding sensual pleasures are not well abandoned and not well calmed internally—even if those good ascetics and brahmins feel painful, sharp, severe, bitter *vedanā* brought about by exertion, they are incapable of knowledge and vision and unsurpassed awakening. And even if those good ascetics and brahmins do not feel painful, sharp, severe, bitter *vedanā* brought about by exertion, they are still incapable of knowledge and vision and unsurpassed awakening.
mn36:18.12: This was the second simile that occurred to me, Aggivessana, never heard before, astonishing.
mn36:18.2: Suppose, Aggivessana, there were wet, sappy wood lying on dry land, far from the water.
mn36:18.3: Then a man might come, taking an upper fire-stick:
mn36:18.4: ‘I will kindle fire, I will bring forth heat.’
mn36:18.6: Could that man, by rubbing that wet, sappy wood lying on dry land far from the water with an upper fire-stick, kindle fire or bring forth heat?”
mn36:18.9: “Because, Master Gotama, that is wet, sappy wood, even though it is lying on dry land far from the water.
mn36:19.1: And then a third simile occurred to me, Aggivessana, never heard before, astonishing.
mn36:19.10: “In the same way, Aggivessana, whatever ascetics or brahmins dwell withdrawn from sensual pleasures in body and mind, and whose sensual desire, sensual affection, sensual infatuation, sensual thirst, and sensual fever regarding sensual pleasures are well abandoned and well calmed internally—even if those good ascetics and brahmins feel painful, sharp, severe, bitter *vedanā* brought about by exertion, they are capable of knowledge and vision and unsurpassed awakening. And even if those good ascetics and brahmins do not feel painful, sharp, severe, bitter *vedanā* brought about by exertion, they are still capable of knowledge and vision and unsurpassed awakening.
mn36:19.11: This was the third simile that occurred to me, Aggivessana, never heard before, astonishing.
mn36:19.12: These three similes occurred to me, Aggivessana, never heard before, astonishing.
mn36:19.2: Suppose, Aggivessana, there were dry wood, free of sap, lying on dry land far from the water.
mn36:19.3: Then a man might come, taking an upper fire-stick:
mn36:19.4: ‘I will kindle fire, I will bring forth heat.’
mn36:19.6: Could that man, by rubbing that dry, sapless wood lying on dry land far from the water with an upper fire-stick, kindle fire and bring forth heat?”
mn36:19.9: “Because, Master Gotama, that is dry, sapless wood, and it is lying on dry land far from the water.”
mn36:2.1: Now at that time, in the morning the *Bhagavā* had dressed and, taking robe and bowl, wanted to enter Vesālī for alms.
mn36:20.1: Then this thought occurred to me, Aggivessana:
mn36:20.2: ‘What if, pressing my teeth against my teeth, pressing my tongue against the roof of my mouth, I were to restrain, squeeze, and scorch the mind [*citta*] with my mind?’
mn36:20.3: Then, Aggivessana, pressing my teeth against my teeth, pressing my tongue against the roof of my mouth, I restrained, squeezed, and scorched the mind [*citta*] with my mind.
mn36:20.4: While I was thus pressing my teeth against my teeth, pressing my tongue against the roof of my mouth, restraining, squeezing, and scorching the mind [*citta*] with my mind, sweat poured from my armpits.
mn36:20.5: Just as a strong man might seize a weaker man by the head or shoulders and restrain, squeeze, and scorch him,
mn36:20.6: so too, Aggivessana, while I was pressing my teeth against my teeth, pressing my tongue against the roof of my mouth, restraining, squeezing, and scorching the mind [*citta*] with my mind, sweat poured from my armpits.
mn36:20.7: But my energy was aroused, not slackened; my *sati* was established, not lost. Yet my body was tense, not calmed, because I was exhausted by that painful striving.
mn36:20.8: Even such painful *vedanā* as arose in me, Aggivessana, did not take possession of my mind [*citta*].
mn36:21.1: Then this thought occurred to me, Aggivessana:
mn36:21.2: ‘What if I were to meditate [*jhāyeyyaṁ*] only in a breathless *jhāna* [*appāṇakaṁyeva jhānaṁ*]?’
mn36:21.3: Then, Aggivessana, I stopped the in-breaths and out-breaths through my mouth and nose.
mn36:21.4: While the in-breaths and out-breaths through my mouth and nose were stopped, there was a loud sound of winds coming out through my ear passages.
mn36:21.5: Just as there is a loud sound when a smith’s bellows are blown,
mn36:21.6: so too, Aggivessana, while the in-breaths and out-breaths through my mouth and nose were stopped, there was a loud sound of winds coming out through my ear passages.
mn36:21.7: But my energy was aroused, not slackened; my *sati* was established, not lost. Yet my body was tense, not calmed, because I was exhausted by that painful striving.
mn36:21.8: Even such painful *vedanā* as arose in me, Aggivessana, did not take possession of my mind [*citta*].
mn36:22.1: Then this thought occurred to me, Aggivessana:
mn36:22.2: ‘What if I were to meditate [*jhāyeyyaṁ*] only in a breathless *jhāna* [*appāṇakaṁyeva jhānaṁ*]?’
mn36:22.3: Then, Aggivessana, I stopped the in-breaths and out-breaths through my mouth, nose, and ears.
mn36:22.4: While the in-breaths and out-breaths through my mouth, nose, and ears were stopped, violent winds battered my head.
mn36:22.5: Just as a strong man might strike the head with a sharp sword-point,
mn36:22.6: so too, Aggivessana, while the in-breaths and out-breaths through my mouth, nose, and ears were stopped, violent winds battered my head.
mn36:22.7: But my energy was aroused, not slackened; my *sati* was established, not lost. Yet my body was tense, not calmed, because I was exhausted by that painful striving.
mn36:22.8: Even such painful *vedanā* as arose in me, Aggivessana, did not take possession of my mind [*citta*].
mn36:23.1: Then this thought occurred to me, Aggivessana:
mn36:23.2: ‘What if I were to meditate [*jhāyeyyaṁ*] only in a breathless *jhāna* [*appāṇakaṁyeva jhānaṁ*]?’
mn36:23.3: Then, Aggivessana, I stopped the in-breaths and out-breaths through my mouth, nose, and ears.
mn36:23.4: While the in-breaths and out-breaths through my mouth, nose, and ears were stopped, intense pains arose in my head.
mn36:23.5: Just as a strong man might bind the head tight with a tough leather strap,
mn36:23.6: so too, Aggivessana, while the in-breaths and out-breaths through my mouth, nose, and ears were stopped, intense pains arose in my head.
mn36:23.7: But my energy was aroused, not slackened; my *sati* was established, not lost. Yet my body was tense, not calmed, because I was exhausted by that painful striving.
mn36:23.8: Even such painful *vedanā* as arose in me, Aggivessana, did not take possession of my mind [*citta*].
mn36:24.1: Then this thought occurred to me, Aggivessana:
mn36:24.2: ‘What if I were to meditate [*jhāyeyyaṁ*] only in a breathless *jhāna* [*appāṇakaṁyeva jhānaṁ*]?’
mn36:24.3: Then, Aggivessana, I stopped the in-breaths and out-breaths through my mouth, nose, and ears.
mn36:24.4: While the in-breaths and out-breaths through my mouth, nose, and ears were stopped, violent winds cut through my belly.
mn36:24.5: Just as a skilled butcher or butcher’s apprentice might carve open the belly with a sharp carving knife,
mn36:24.6: so too, Aggivessana, while the in-breaths and out-breaths through my mouth, nose, and ears were stopped, violent winds cut through my belly.
mn36:24.7: But my energy was aroused, not slackened; my *sati* was established, not lost. Yet my body was tense, not calmed, because I was exhausted by that painful striving.
mn36:24.8: Even such painful *vedanā* as arose in me, Aggivessana, did not take possession of my mind [*citta*].
mn36:25.1: Then this thought occurred to me, Aggivessana:
mn36:25.2: ‘What if I were to meditate [*jhāyeyyaṁ*] only in a breathless *jhāna* [*appāṇakaṁyeva jhānaṁ*]?’
mn36:25.3: Then, Aggivessana, I stopped the in-breaths and out-breaths through my mouth, nose, and ears.
mn36:25.4: While the in-breaths and out-breaths through my mouth, nose, and ears were stopped, there was an intense burning in my body.
mn36:25.5: Just as two strong men might seize a weaker man by both arms and roast and scorch him over a pit of burning coals,
mn36:25.6: so too, Aggivessana, while the in-breaths and out-breaths through my mouth, nose, and ears were stopped, there was an intense burning in my body.
mn36:25.7: But my energy was aroused, not slackened; my *sati* was established, not lost. Yet my body was tense, not calmed, because I was exhausted by that painful striving.
mn36:25.8: Even such painful *vedanā* as arose in me, Aggivessana, did not take possession of my mind [*citta*].
mn36:26.1: And yet, Aggivessana, some *devatās*, seeing me, said this:
mn36:26.4: ‘The ascetic Gotama is not dead, but is dying.’
mn36:26.6: ‘The ascetic Gotama is neither dead nor dying; the ascetic Gotama is an *arahant*; this is simply the sort of dwelling of an *arahant*.’
mn36:27.1: Then this thought occurred to me, Aggivessana:
mn36:27.2: ‘What if I were to undertake complete abstention from food?’
mn36:27.3: Then, Aggivessana, *devatās* approached me and said this:
mn36:27.4: ‘Please do not, sir, undertake complete abstention from food.
mn36:27.5: If you do undertake complete abstention from food, sir, we will infuse divine nourishment into you through the hair-pores, and by that you will keep yourself alive.’
mn36:27.6: Then this thought occurred to me, Aggivessana:
mn36:27.7: ‘If I were to claim total non-eating, while these *devatās* infused divine nourishment into me through the hair-pores and I lived on that, it would be false on my part.’
mn36:27.8: Then, Aggivessana, I dismissed those *devatās*, saying, ‘Enough.’
mn36:28.1: Then this thought occurred to me, Aggivessana:
mn36:28.10: The skin of my head became shriveled and withered like a bitter gourd cut unripe, shriveled by wind and sun, because of eating so little.
mn36:28.11: Then, Aggivessana, if I thought, ‘I will touch the skin of my belly,’ I would seize only my backbone; and if I thought, ‘I will touch my backbone,’ I would seize only the skin of my belly. So closely had the skin of my belly clung to my backbone because of eating so little.
mn36:28.12: Then, Aggivessana, when I thought, ‘I will relieve myself, whether urine or excrement,’ I would fall over right there on my face because of eating so little.
mn36:28.13: Then, Aggivessana, in order to soothe this very body, I rubbed my limbs with my hand. While I rubbed my limbs with my hand, the body hairs, rotten at the roots, fell from my body because of eating so little.
mn36:28.2: ‘What if I were to take a little food, a few spoonfuls—whether bean broth, horsegram broth, pea broth, or vetch broth?’
mn36:28.3: Then, Aggivessana, I took a little food, a few spoonfuls—whether bean broth, horsegram broth, pea broth, or vetch broth.
mn36:28.4: While I was taking a little food, a few spoonfuls—whether bean broth, horsegram broth, pea broth, or vetch broth—my body reached an extreme degree of emaciation.
mn36:28.5: My limbs became like the segmented joints of withered creepers because of eating so little.
mn36:28.6: My buttocks became like a camel’s hoofprint because of eating so little.
mn36:28.7: My backbone stood out high and low like a string of beads because of eating so little.
mn36:28.8: My ribs stuck out jutting and sagging like the rafters of an old shed because of eating so little.
mn36:28.9: The pupils of my eyes appeared sunk deep in their sockets like water-stars seen deep down in a well because of eating so little.
mn36:29.1: And yet, Aggivessana, when people saw me, they said: ‘The ascetic Gotama is black.’
mn36:29.2: Some people said: ‘The ascetic Gotama is not black; he is brown.’
mn36:29.3: Some people said: ‘The ascetic Gotama is neither black nor brown; he has the skin of a dark fish.’
mn36:29.4: So far had my naturally pure bright complexion been ruined because of eating so little.
mn36:3.1: Then Saccaka, the Nigaṇṭha’s son, while walking and wandering for exercise, approached the Great Wood, the hall with the peaked roof.
mn36:3.2: Venerable Ānanda saw Saccaka, the Nigaṇṭha’s son, coming from afar.
mn36:3.3: Seeing him, he said this to the *Bhagavā*:
mn36:3.4: “*Bhante*, this Saccaka, the Nigaṇṭha’s son, is coming—an eloquent debater, one who speaks as though learned, well regarded by many people.
mn36:3.5: He wants to speak against the Buddha, he wants to speak against the *dhamma*, he wants to speak against the *saṅgha*.
mn36:3.6: It would be good, *bhante*, if the *Bhagavā*, out of compassion, were to sit down for a moment.”
mn36:3.7: The *Bhagavā* sat down on the seat laid out.
mn36:3.8: Then Saccaka, the Nigaṇṭha’s son, approached the *Bhagavā*; having approached, he exchanged greetings with the *Bhagavā*. After exchanging courteous and memorable talk, he sat down to one side. Sitting to one side, Saccaka, the Nigaṇṭha’s son, said this to the *Bhagavā*:
mn36:30.1: Then this thought occurred to me, Aggivessana:
mn36:30.2: ‘Whatever ascetics or brahmins in the past have felt painful, sharp, severe, bitter *vedanā* brought about by exertion—this is the utmost; it goes no further.
mn36:30.3: Whatever ascetics or brahmins in the future will feel painful, sharp, severe, bitter *vedanā* brought about by exertion—this is the utmost; it will go no further.
mn36:30.4: Whatever ascetics or brahmins at present feel painful, sharp, severe, bitter *vedanā* brought about by exertion—this is the utmost; it goes no further.
mn36:30.5: But by this bitter, difficult practice I do not attain any further superhuman distinction in knowledge and vision worthy of the noble ones.
mn36:30.6: Could there be another path to awakening?’
mn36:31.1: Then this thought occurred to me, Aggivessana:
mn36:31.2: ‘I do recall that while my father the Sakyan was working, as I was sitting in the cool shade of a rose-apple tree, quite secluded from sensual pleasures, secluded from unskillful *dhammas*, I entered and dwelled in the first *jhāna*, which has thought [*savitakkaṁ*] and examination [*savicāraṁ*], with *pīti* and *sukha* born of seclusion [*vivekajaṁ pītisukhaṁ*].
mn36:31.3: Could that be the path to awakening?’
mn36:31.4: Then, Aggivessana, a consciousness [*viññāṇaṁ*] following on that memory arose in me:
mn36:31.5: ‘That indeed is the path to awakening.’
mn36:32.1: Then this thought occurred to me, Aggivessana:
mn36:32.2: ‘Why am I afraid of that *sukha*, which is apart from sensual pleasures and apart from unskillful *dhammas*?’
mn36:32.3: Then this thought occurred to me, Aggivessana:
mn36:32.4: ‘I am not afraid of that *sukha*, which is apart from sensual pleasures and apart from unskillful *dhammas*.’
mn36:33.1: Then this thought occurred to me, Aggivessana:
mn36:33.2: ‘It is not easy to attain that *sukha* with a body that has reached such an extreme of emaciation. What if I were to take coarse food—boiled rice and porridge?’
mn36:33.3: Then, Aggivessana, I took coarse food—boiled rice and porridge.
mn36:33.4: Now at that time, Aggivessana, five *bhikkhus* were attending on me:
mn36:33.5: ‘Whatever *dhamma* the ascetic Gotama attains, he will tell us.’
mn36:33.6: But when, Aggivessana, I took coarse food—boiled rice and porridge—those five *bhikkhus*, becoming disenchanted, left me:
mn36:33.7: ‘The ascetic Gotama lives luxuriously; he has given up striving and has reverted to abundance.’
mn36:34.1: Then, Aggivessana, after taking coarse food and gaining strength, quite secluded from sensual pleasures, secluded from unskillful *dhammas*, I entered and dwelled in the first *jhāna*, which has thought [*savitakkaṁ*] and examination [*savicāraṁ*], with *pīti* and *sukha* born of seclusion [*vivekajaṁ pītisukhaṁ*].
mn36:34.2: Even such pleasant *vedanā* as arose in me, Aggivessana, did not take possession of my mind [*citta*].
mn36:35-37.1: With the settling of thought [*vitakka*] and examination [*vicāra*], with internal confidence and unification [*ekodibhāvaṁ*] of mind, without thought [*avitakkaṁ*] and without examination [*avicāraṁ*], with *pīti* and *sukha* born of *samādhi* [*samādhijaṁ pītisukhaṁ*], I entered and dwelled in the second *jhāna*.
mn36:35-37.2: Even such pleasant *vedanā* as arose in me, Aggivessana, did not take possession of my mind [*citta*].
mn36:35-37.3: And with the fading of *pīti*, I dwelled in equanimity [*upekkhā*], with *sati* and fully aware [*sampajāno*], and I experienced *sukha* with the body—of which the noble ones declare: ‘One who is equanimous [*upekkhako*], with *sati*, dwells in *sukha*’—I entered and dwelled in the third *jhāna*.
mn36:35-37.4: Even such pleasant *vedanā* as arose in me, Aggivessana, did not take possession of my mind [*citta*].
mn36:35-37.5: With the giving up of *sukha* and the giving up of *dukkha*, and with the earlier disappearance of joy and distress, I entered and dwelled in the fourth *jhāna*, which is neither-*dukkha*-nor-*sukha*, purified by equanimity [*upekkhā*] and *sati* [*upekkhāsatipārisuddhiṁ*].
mn36:35-37.6: Even such pleasant *vedanā* as arose in me, Aggivessana, did not take possession of my mind [*citta*].
mn36:38.1: When my mind [*citta*] was thus endowed with *samādhi* [*samāhite citte*], purified, bright, stainless, rid of defilements, pliant, workable, steady, and attained to imperturbability, I directed the mind [*citta*] to the knowledge of recollecting former lives.
mn36:38.2: I recollected my many kinds of former lives, that is: one birth … Thus with features and details I recollected my many kinds of former lives.
mn36:39.1: This, Aggivessana, was the first knowledge attained by me in the first watch of the night;
mn36:39.2: blindness [*avijjā*] was destroyed, knowledge arose; darkness was destroyed, light arose—as happens for one dwelling diligent, ardent, and resolute.
mn36:39.3: Even such pleasant *vedanā* as arose in me, Aggivessana, did not take possession of my mind [*citta*].
mn36:4.1: “There are, Master Gotama, some ascetics and brahmins who dwell devoted to the practice of development of the body [*kāyabhāvanā*], but not development of mind [*cittabhāvanaṁ*].
mn36:4.10: His body follows the mind [*citta*]; it proceeds according to the mind [*citta*].
mn36:4.12: Because the body has not been developed [*abhāvitattā kāyassa*].
mn36:4.13: So it occurs to me, Master Gotama:
mn36:4.14: ‘Surely Master Gotama’s disciples dwell devoted to the practice of development of mind [*cittabhāvanā*], not development of the body [*kāyabhāvanaṁ*].’”
mn36:4.3: It has happened, Master Gotama, that when someone was touched by a painful bodily *vedanā*, his thighs might go rigid, his heart might burst, hot blood might gush from his mouth, and he might fall into madness and mental derangement.
mn36:4.4: His mind [*citta*] follows the body; it proceeds according to the body.
mn36:4.6: Because the mind [*citta*] has not been developed [*abhāvitattā cittassa*].
mn36:4.7: But there are, Master Gotama, some ascetics and brahmins who dwell devoted to the practice of development of mind [*cittabhāvanā*], but not development of the body [*kāyabhāvanaṁ*].
mn36:4.9: It has happened, Master Gotama, that when someone was touched by a painful mental *vedanā*, his thighs might go rigid, his heart might burst, hot blood might gush from his mouth, and he might fall into madness and mental derangement.
mn36:40.1: When my mind [*citta*] was thus endowed with *samādhi* [*samāhite citte*], purified, bright, stainless, rid of defilements, pliant, workable, steady, and attained to imperturbability, I directed the mind [*citta*] to the knowledge of the passing away and reappearance of beings.
mn36:40.2: With the divine eye, purified and surpassing the human, I saw beings passing away and reappearing, inferior and superior, beautiful and ugly, in good destinations and bad, and I understood beings according to their kamma …
mn36:41.1: this, Aggivessana, was the second knowledge attained by me in the middle watch of the night;
mn36:41.2: blindness [*avijjā*] was destroyed, knowledge arose; darkness was destroyed, light arose—as happens for one dwelling diligent, ardent, and resolute.
mn36:41.3: Even such pleasant *vedanā* as arose in me, Aggivessana, did not take possession of my mind [*citta*].
mn36:42.1: When my mind [*citta*] was thus endowed with *samādhi* [*samāhite citte*], purified, bright, stainless, rid of defilements, pliant, workable, steady, and attained to imperturbability, I directed the mind [*citta*] to the knowledge of the ending of the *āsavas*.
mn36:42.2: I directly knew as it truly is: ‘This is *dukkha*’; I directly knew as it truly is: ‘This is the arising of *dukkha*’; I directly knew as it truly is: ‘This is the cessation of *dukkha*’; I directly knew as it truly is: ‘This is the way leading to the cessation of *dukkha*.’
mn36:42.3: I directly knew as it truly is: ‘These are the *āsavas*’; I directly knew as it truly is: ‘This is the arising of the *āsavas*’; I directly knew as it truly is: ‘This is the cessation of the *āsavas*’; I directly knew as it truly is: ‘This is the way leading to the cessation of the *āsavas*.’
mn36:43.1: For me, knowing thus and seeing thus, the mind [*citta*] was liberated from the *āsava* of sensuality [*kāmāsavā*], from the *āsava* of *bhava* [*bhavāsavā*], and from the *āsava* of blindness [*avijjā*] [*avijjāsavā*].
mn36:43.2: When it was liberated, there came the knowledge: ‘It is liberated.’
mn36:43.3: I directly knew: ‘Birth is ended, the holy life has been lived, what had to be done has been done, there is no more of this state of being.’
mn36:44.1: This, Aggivessana, was the third knowledge attained by me in the last watch of the night;
mn36:44.2: blindness [*avijjā*] was destroyed, knowledge arose; darkness was destroyed, light arose—as happens for one dwelling diligent, ardent, and resolute.
mn36:44.3: Even such pleasant *vedanā* as arose in me, Aggivessana, did not take possession of my mind [*citta*].
mn36:45.1: And I do remember, Aggivessana, teaching *dhamma* to an assembly of many hundreds.
mn36:45.2: Yet each one among them thinks this:
mn36:45.3: ‘The ascetic Gotama teaches *dhamma* with me alone as his concern.’
mn36:45.4: But, Aggivessana, it should not be seen in that way.
mn36:45.5: The Tathāgata teaches *dhamma* to others only for the sake of making it known.
mn36:45.6: At the end of that very talk, I steady the mind [*citta*], settle it, make it unified [*ekodiṁ*], and collect it inwards on that very same earlier *nimitta* of *samādhi* [*samādhinimitte*], in which I dwell constantly.”
mn36:45.7: “This is believable of Master Gotama, as of an *arahant*, a perfectly awakened Buddha.
mn36:45.8: But does Master Gotama acknowledge sleeping during the day?”
mn36:46.1: “I do acknowledge, Aggivessana, that in the last month of the hot season, after the meal, having returned from almsround, I spread out the outer robe folded in four, lay down on my right side, with *sati*, fully aware [*sampajāno*], and fell asleep.”
mn36:46.2: “That, Master Gotama, is what some ascetics and brahmins call dwelling in delusion.”
mn36:46.3: “Not merely by that much, Aggivessana, is one deluded or not deluded.
mn36:46.4: Rather, Aggivessana, listen to how one is deluded and how one is not deluded.
mn36:46.5: Listen to that, pay careful attention, and I will speak.”
mn36:46.6: “Yes, sir,” Saccaka, the Nigaṇṭha’s son, replied to the *Bhagavā*.
mn36:47.1: “Whoever, Aggivessana, has those *āsavas* that defile, lead to renewed *bhava*, are troublesome, ripen in *dukkha*, and lead on to future birth, old age, and death, not abandoned—him I call ‘deluded.’
mn36:47.2: For it is through the non-abandoning of the *āsavas*, Aggivessana, that one is deluded.
mn36:47.3: Whoever, Aggivessana, has those *āsavas* that defile, lead to renewed *bhava*, are troublesome, ripen in *dukkha*, and lead on to future birth, old age, and death, abandoned—him I call ‘not deluded.’
mn36:47.4: For it is through the abandoning of the *āsavas*, Aggivessana, that one is not deluded.
mn36:47.5: As for the Tathāgata, Aggivessana, those *āsavas* that defile, lead to renewed *bhava*, are troublesome, ripen in *dukkha*, and lead on to future birth, old age, and death have been abandoned, cut off at the root, made like a palm stump, brought to non-existence, not liable to arise again in the future.
mn36:47.6: Just as, Aggivessana, a palm tree with its crown cut off is incapable of growing again,
mn36:47.7: so too, Aggivessana, for the Tathāgata those *āsavas* that defile, lead to renewed *bhava*, are troublesome, ripen in *dukkha*, and lead on to future birth, old age, and death have been abandoned, cut off at the root, made like a palm stump, brought to non-existence, not liable to arise again in the future.”
mn36:48.1: When this was said, Saccaka, the Nigaṇṭha’s son, said this to the *Bhagavā*:
mn36:48.12: When I had entered into debate with him, he dodged from one point to another, led the discussion aside, and displayed anger, hatred, and displeasure.
mn36:48.13: But when Master Gotama is spoken to again and again in this way, addressed with pressing lines of speech, his complexion grows all the more clear and the color of his face becomes serene, as is fitting for an *arahant*, a perfectly awakened Buddha.
mn36:48.14: Well then, Master Gotama, we will go now.
mn36:48.15: We have many duties, much to do.”
mn36:48.16: “Now is the time for you to do as you think fit, Aggivessana.”
mn36:48.17: Then Saccaka, the Nigaṇṭha’s son, having delighted in and approved of what the *Bhagavā* said, rose from his seat and departed.
mn36:48.18: The Greater Discourse to Saccaka is finished, the sixth.
mn36:48.2: “It is wonderful, Master Gotama, it is amazing, Master Gotama.
mn36:48.3: How remarkable it is that when Master Gotama is spoken to again and again in this way, addressed with pressing lines of speech, his complexion grows all the more clear and the color of his face becomes serene, as is fitting for an *arahant*, a perfectly awakened Buddha.
mn36:48.4: I remember entering into debate with Pūraṇa Kassapa.
mn36:48.5: When I had entered into debate with him, he dodged from one point to another, led the discussion aside, and displayed anger, hatred, and displeasure.
mn36:48.6: But when Master Gotama is spoken to again and again in this way, addressed with pressing lines of speech, his complexion grows all the more clear and the color of his face becomes serene, as is fitting for an *arahant*, a perfectly awakened Buddha.
mn36:48.7: I remember entering into debate with Makkhali Gosāla …
mn36:5.1: “But what, Aggivessana, have you heard to be development of the body [*kāyabhāvanā*]?”
mn36:5.3: Nanda Vaccha, Kisa Saṅkicca, and Makkhali Gosāla—
mn36:5.4: these naked ascetics, Master Gotama, unrestrained in conduct, licking their hands clean, not coming when asked to come, not staying when asked to stay, do not accept food brought to them, food prepared specially for them, or an invitation.
mn36:5.5: They do not accept from the mouth of a pot, they do not accept from the mouth of a bowl, nor from between doorposts, from between sticks, from between pestles, from two people eating, from a pregnant woman, from a nursing woman, from a woman in the company of a man, from where food is announced for distribution, from where a dog is standing by, or from where flies swarm. They do not eat fish or meat, and they do not drink liquor, wine, or fermented rice-water.
mn36:5.6: They may go to one house only and take one morsel, or to two houses and take two morsels … or to seven houses and take seven morsels.
mn36:5.7: They keep themselves going on one gift of food, on two gifts … on seven gifts.
mn36:5.8: They eat once a day, once every two days … once every seven days. Thus they dwell devoted to the practice of taking food at intervals, even for a fortnight at a time.”
mn36:6.1: “But do they live on just that much, Aggivessana?”
mn36:6.3: Sometimes, Master Gotama, they eat very fine solid foods, consume very fine meals, enjoy very fine beds, and drink very fine drinks.
mn36:6.4: Thus they restore this body’s strength, build it up, and fatten it.”
mn36:6.5: “Aggivessana, what they first give up and later build up again—thus there is growth and decline in this body.
mn36:6.6: But what have you heard to be development of mind [*cittabhāvanā*]?”
mn36:6.7: When Saccaka, the Nigaṇṭha’s son, was asked by the *Bhagavā* about development of mind [*cittabhāvanāya*], he was unable to answer.
mn36:7.1: Then the *Bhagavā* said this to Saccaka, the Nigaṇṭha’s son:
mn36:7.2: “And even that former development of the body [*kāyabhāvanā*] that you spoke of, Aggivessana, is not righteous development of the body [*kāyabhāvanā*] in the noble one’s *vinaya*.
mn36:7.3: You do not even understand development of the body [*kāyabhāvanaṁ*], Aggivessana, so how will you understand development of mind [*cittabhāvanaṁ*]?
mn36:7.4: Still, Aggivessana, listen to how one is with undeveloped body [*abhāvitakāyo*] and undeveloped mind [*abhāvitacitto*], and with developed body [*bhāvitakāyo*] and developed mind [*bhāvitacitto*].
mn36:7.5: Listen to that, pay careful attention, and I will speak.”
mn36:7.6: “Yes, sir,” Saccaka, the Nigaṇṭha’s son, replied to the *Bhagavā*.
mn36:8.1: “And how, Aggivessana, is one with undeveloped body [*abhāvitakāyo*] and undeveloped mind [*abhāvitacitto*]?
mn36:8.2: Here, Aggivessana, a pleasant *vedanā* arises in an uninstructed ordinary person.
mn36:8.3: Touched by that pleasant *vedanā*, he becomes attached to pleasantness and falls into attachment to pleasantness.
mn36:8.5: With the ceasing of the pleasant *vedanā*, a painful *vedanā* arises.
mn36:8.6: Touched by that painful *vedanā*, he grieves, grows weary, laments, beats his breast and cries out, and falls into bewilderment.
mn36:8.7: That pleasant *vedanā*, Aggivessana, even when arisen, takes possession of his mind [*citta*] because the body has not been developed [*abhāvitattā kāyassa*]; and that painful *vedanā*, even when arisen, takes possession of his mind [*citta*] because the mind [*citta*] has not been developed [*abhāvitattā cittassa*].
mn36:8.8: Whoever is like this in both respects—so that even arisen pleasant *vedanā* takes possession of the mind [*citta*] because the body has not been developed [*abhāvitattā kāyassa*], and even arisen painful *vedanā* takes possession of the mind [*citta*] because the mind [*citta*] has not been developed [*abhāvitattā cittassa*]—that is how one is with undeveloped body [*abhāvitakāyo*] and undeveloped mind [*abhāvitacitto*].
mn36:9.1: And how, Aggivessana, is one with developed body [*bhāvitakāyo*] and developed mind [*bhāvitacitto*]?
mn36:9.2: Here, Aggivessana, a pleasant *vedanā* arises in an instructed noble disciple.
mn36:9.3: Touched by that pleasant *vedanā*, he does not become attached to pleasantness, and he does not fall into attachment to pleasantness.
mn36:9.5: With the ceasing of the pleasant *vedanā*, a painful *vedanā* arises.
mn36:9.6: Touched by that painful *vedanā*, he does not grieve, does not grow weary, does not lament, does not beat his breast and cry out, and does not fall into bewilderment.
mn36:9.7: That pleasant *vedanā*, Aggivessana, even when arisen, does not take possession of his mind [*citta*] because the body has been developed [*bhāvitattā kāyassa*]; and that painful *vedanā*, even when arisen, does not take possession of his mind [*citta*] because the mind [*citta*] has been developed [*bhāvitattā cittassa*].
mn36:9.8: Whoever is like this in both respects—so that even arisen pleasant *vedanā* does not take possession of the mind [*citta*] because the body has been developed [*bhāvitattā kāyassa*], and even arisen painful *vedanā* does not take possession of the mind [*citta*] because the mind [*citta*] has been developed [*bhāvitattā cittassa*]—that is how one is with developed body [*bhāvitakāyo*] and developed mind [*bhāvitacitto*].”
